Issues.Title: Port first-run experience to the NativeAOT CLI entrypoint
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Continues the AOT `dotnet` CLI arc by bringing the cross-cutting **first-run experience** — previously implemented only in the managed `Program.cs` — to the NativeAOT entrypoint, and aligns the two entrypoints structurally so parity is easy to verify.

This is being opened to exercise the changes in CI (notably the AOT publish/test legs across platforms).

## What's here

- **Shared first-run logic.** Extract the first-run experience into `src/Cli/dotnet/FirstRunExperience.cs`, used by both the managed CLI and the AOT bridge (`NativeEntryPoint`), instead of living inline in `Program.cs`.
- **Shared command invocation.** Extract the in-process command invocation into `CommandInvocation.ExecuteInternalCommand` so `NativeEntryPoint` no longer references `Program`. Both entrypoints call the same method.
- **In-process workload detection.** `WorkloadInstallDetector` detects installed workloads in-process by **reusing** the existing `RegistryWorkloadInstallationRecordRepository` read path. The elevation/MSI-IPC write members and `InstallerBase` are `#if`'d away for AOT rather than reimplemented. The AOT first run now only defers to the managed CLI when workload **repair** is actually pending.
- **In-process dev-cert generation on AOT.** Generate the ASP.NET Core HTTPS developer certificate in-process under NativeAOT. The `CertificateManager` uses only BCL X509/RSA crypto (no reflection/dynamic code); on macOS the crypto PAL resolves dynamically against the host's already-loaded native library, so the existing `RemoveAppleCryptoFromNativeAotLink` workaround is preserved.
- **No `Program.cs` in dotnet-aot.** `dotnet-aot` is a `NativeLib=Shared` library whose entry points are the `[UnmanagedCallersOnly]` exports in `NativeEntryPoint` — it never invoked a managed `Program.Main`, so the linked managed `Program.cs` is dropped entirely (not replaced with a project-local one). The managed `dotnet.dll` keeps its own `Program.cs` (used by the hostfxr fallback path), with the now-dead `#if CLI_AOT` branches removed. `DESIGN.md` updated to match.

## Validation (local, Windows)

- AOT, managed CLI, and AOT test builds: **0 warnings / 0 errors**.
- AOT publish (full ILC native codegen, with no `Program.cs`): **0 warnings / 0 errors** — no IL2026/IL3050.
- AOT test suite: **77 passed / 6 skipped / 0 failed** (the 6 skip because the AOT `dn` binary isn't built in Debug).

## AOT size impact

Measured by publishing `dotnet-aot` (Release, `win-x64`, full ILC) on this branch vs. the PR base commit (`793ebf24`), then diffing the `dotnet-aot.mstat` files with `sizoscope-cli` (same methodology as `.github/workflows/aot-size-analysis.yml`).

| Native `dotnet-aot.dll` | Size |
| --- | --- |
| Base (`793ebf24`) | 16.846 MB (17,664,000 B) |
| This PR | 17.438 MB (18,285,056 B) |
| **Delta** | **+606 KB (+3.52%)** |

`sizoscope-cli` accounted difference: **+490.7 kB**, attributable entirely to the first-run features ported in-process:

| Contributor | Δ | Reason |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `System.Security.Cryptography` | +133.1 kB | dev-cert generation |
| `System.Private.CoreLib` | +120 kB | crypto/registry surface |
| `System.Security.AccessControl` | +39.0 kB | registry ACL read |
| `System.Linq` | +18.5 kB | detection/cert code |
| `Microsoft.AspNetCore.DeveloperCertificates.XPlat` | +15.8 kB | cert generator package |
| `System.Formats.Asn1` | +9.5 kB | cert encoding |
| `System.Security.Principal.Windows` | +8.6 kB | registry identity |
| `Microsoft.Deployment.DotNet.Releases` | +6.7 kB | workload band detection |
| `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.WorkloadManifestReader` | +5.6 kB | workload detection |
| `NuGet.Common` / `Cli.Utils` / `Configurer` / `Win32.Registry` | ~11 kB | workload record repo reuse |

The large `*.resources` rows in the raw `sizoscope` output appear with equal `+`/`−` magnitudes (localized satellites re-attributed across builds) and net to ~zero — which is why the accounted total (490.7 kB) is below the raw file delta (606 kB). No unexpected dependencies were pulled in.

## Notes for reviewers

- The macOS runtime cert path (`/usr/bin/security import` + dynamic crypto PAL resolution with the static archive removed) compiles AOT-clean but could not be runtime-validated locally on Windows — **this PR is partly here to validate that on macOS CI.**

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

Issues.Title: Add dotnetup local SDK setup
Issues.Body:
## Summary
- Adds `dotnetup sdk install --local` / root install `--local` for project-local SDK setup.
- Installs into the nearest project `.dotnet` directory and configures `global.json` with `sdk.paths: [".dotnet", "$host$"]`.
- Preserves existing `global.json` sections, supports exact/channel/prerelease settings, updates `.gitignore` idempotently, and avoids PATH/profile/default install mutations.
- Adds unit, parser, and end-to-end coverage plus docs for the new local install flow.

## Validation
- Built `src\Installer\dotnetup\dotnetup.csproj` with `/p:_DotnetupSkipPublishOnBuild=true`.
- Built `test\dotnetup.Tests\dotnetup.Tests.csproj` with isolated `ArtifactsDir`.
- Ran focused tests: `GlobalJsonUpdateTests`, `ParserTests`, `LibraryTests`, local install E2E, env script E2E, and stdin forwarding E2E.
- Ran manual throwaway-project E2E for exact local install, existing `global.json` preservation, nested global.json discovery, invalid/missing version errors, `.gitignore` newline/idempotence, and preview local install.
- Ran full `dotnetup.Tests`; local result has one pre-existing environment-specific failure in `WindowsPathHelperTests.GetProgramFilesDotnetPaths_ReturnsValidPaths` because this machine returns `C:\Program Files\dotnet\x64`.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Add dotnetup local SDK setup" (#54968)

Issues.Title: Ignore MTP exit code 8 (zero tests ran) for Helix work items
Issues.Body:
## Problem

Some Helix work items fail with **exit code 8** even though the test results tab shows everything green, which is confusing and has led to PRs being merged in a falsely-failing state (see #54963, #54788).

Exit code `8` in Microsoft.Testing.Platform (MTP) means [**"the test session ran zero tests"**](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/core/testing/microsoft-testing-platform-troubleshooting#exit-codes).

## Root cause

The affected assemblies (`dotnet-aot.Tests`, `Microsoft.Win32.Msi.Tests`, …) are all `MSTest.Sdk` → MTP projects. They run through the `isMTPProject` branch in `SDKCustomCreateXUnitWorkItemsWithTestExclusion`, invoked as `dotnet exec <dll> --filter ...`.

A work item ends up running zero tests when:
- class-based sharding (`--filter`) isolates a partition containing only skipped tests, or
- a project legitimately runs no tests on a platform (e.g. Windows-only Msi tests on Linux/macOS).

MTP then returns exit code 8 and Helix treats the nonzero exit as a failure.

## Fix

Add `--ignore-exit-code 8` to the MTP work-item command so "zero tests ran" reports success instead of failing the work item. This is the mitigation called out in the issue.

- Scoped to the MTP branch only — the VSTest `dotnet test` path doesn't use MTP exit codes and is untouched.
- Real failures (exit 2, crashes, setup errors, etc.) still surface.

The stricter long-term fix (relaxing exit 8 on the test-platform side when tests exist but are all skipped) is tracked separately.

Fixes #54963
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Ignore MTP exit code 8 (zero tests ran) for Helix work items" (#54965)

Issues.Title: Disable in-process parallel test execution
Issues.Body:
# Proposal: Disable In-Process Parallel Test Execution ## Summary This proposal recommends disabling in-process parallel test execution in `dotnet/sdk`. The current approach of running tests concurrently within a single process is causing persistent CI instability that imposes a significant tax on the team's productivity. ## Intermittent Test Failures Are Hindering PR Velocity Flaky tests caused by in-process parallelism are the single biggest source of friction in the PR merge workflow today. ### Obscuring signal and creating toil When a PR shows red CI, you cannot tell at a glance whether the failure is something you introduced or a pre-existing intermittent flake. To find out, you have to dig into every failure, examine the test, check if it's a known issue, and compare against other recent runs. This investigation takes time, creates frustration, and is a constant tax on every PR author and reviewer. The `/ba-g` (build analysis gate bypass) mechanism exists specifically to work around this, but its widespread use is a symptom of the problem, not a solution. ### Masking real regressions Because of the volume of noise, real regressions occasionally slip through unnoticed. When every CI run has failures, it becomes statistically inevitable that some genuine issues are overlooked. Not because people are negligent, but because the signal is drowned out by the noise. ### Wasting resources through retries When tests fail due to flakiness rather than a real problem, contributors retry CI, sometimes many times, hoping for a green run. This burns compute resources that are already in short supply and delays merges. ### The data Of the **100 most recently merged PRs** into `main` (2026-06-12 to 2026-06-23): | Classification | Percentage | |:---------------|:-----------| | Truly clean (no failures at all) | 37% | | Known build errors auto-handled | 18% | | Used `/ba-g` to bypass failures | 36% | | Green only after retries | 5% | | Failed CI, no `/ba-g` | 1% | | Other (neutral) | 3% | **Key takeaways:** - **Only 37%** of PRs merge with a genuinely clean CI run - **63% encounter test failures** that are either bypassed, auto-dismissed, or retried away > See [Appendix: PR Data](#appendix-pr-data) for the full list of 100 PRs with links, CI results, and classification. ## CI on `main` Is Consistently Red The `dotnet-sdk-public-ci` pipeline on `main` has a **3.7% pass rate** over the last ~30 days (2026-05-25 to 2026-06-23): only 4 out of 108 builds succeeded. The last green build was on **June 1, 2026**. A consistently red `main` branch means: - **No reliable baseline** to compare PR results against - **Regressions go undetected** because there's no "known good" state ## Known Build Errors Are Underutilized There are currently [23 open Known Build Error issues](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues?q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aopen+label%3A%22Known+Build+Error%22) and [88 total filed historically](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues?q=is%3Aissue+label%3A%22Known+Build+Error%22). However, the 35% `/ba-g` rate above shows that the true scope of intermittent failures far exceeds what is documented. The Known Build Error system is underutilized because: - Defining a correct regex pattern requires careful effort and there is no tooling to assist - Overly broad patterns risk matching unrelated failures, which is dangerous - It is simply easier to `/ba-g` and move on A recent focused effort to properly document Known Build Errors has resulted in **17 issues filed in June 2026 alone**, compared to 0-2 per month for most of the prior year. This uptick is the result of actively investigating and cataloging the problem, not a sudden increase in flakiness. ## Root Cause: Writing Concurrent Test Code Is Difficult Writing test infrastructure that correctly supports concurrency is inherently difficult. The issues are endless and often difficult to diagnose. The test failures are not product code bugs. The problem is that there are no guardrails that promote writing concurrency-safe test code. It is easy for test authors to follow existing patterns that work in isolation but fail under parallel execution, and these issues are rarely surfaced locally. Retrofitting concurrency safety across the test codebase is an unbounded effort. ### Common patterns | Pattern | Description | |---------|-------------| | Shared file contention | Multiple tests write to the same temp files, binlogs, or solution files | | Resource cleanup races | One test cleans up shared resources while another is still using them | | Thread pool starvation | Leaked threads from watchers/pollers exhaust the thread pool | | Timing-dependent assertions | Tests rely on filesystem notifications or async coordination that breaks under load | | Directory traversal pollution | Tests discover files created by concurrent tests via parent directory walks | ### Illustrative example: [#54942](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/54942) `PostActionTests.AddProjectToSolution_WithOutputRelativePath` fails intermittently because: 1. Test A creates a `.sln` file at the sandbox root directory using a temporary name (`GetTempName()`) 2. Test B, working in a subdirectory, discovers that `.sln` via solution discovery walking up the directory tree 3. Test A finishes and deletes its `.sln` 4. Test B tries to operate on the now-deleted `.sln` . **Failure** Key observations: - **The failing test is doing nothing wrong.** It's a different concurrent test that creates the conflict. - **Diagnosis is extremely difficult.** Resources use `GetTempName()` rather than being named in context of the owning test. There is no way to trace an artifact back to the test that created it. You cannot identify the offending test without first refactoring how artifacts are named across the codebase. - **These are not rare edge cases.** This pattern repeats across the entire test suite with variations on the theme. ### Additional examples | Issue | Problem | |-------|---------| | [#54942](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/54942) | Solution file at sandbox root found by concurrent test via directory traversal, then deleted | | [#54817](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/54817) | `IOException` from concurrent access to `msbuild.binlog` file | | [#54176](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/54176) | Leaked `PollingDirectoryWatcher` threads cause thread pool starvation and hangs | | [#53661](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/53661) | Hot-reload file change detection races assertion timeout under concurrent load | | [#53870](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/53870) | Locked-file warning emission is timing-sensitive; sometimes sees 0 warnings | | [#44878](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/44878) | VS instance discovery has unintended concurrent access to a critical section | | [#9673](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/9673) | Restore tests had to serialize because parallel network operations caused flakiness | ### Why these are not fixable at scale - **Shared global/process resources:** Files, caches, watchers, thread pool, VS discovery, all shared by default. - **Async timing depends on machine load:** Filesystem events, thread scheduling, and network operations behave differently under contention. These failures rarely reproduce locally, which is how the problem grew undetected for so long. - **Tests assume isolation:** Many tests were written assuming sequential execution; they share implicit state without knowing it. - **Diagnosis requires refactoring:** You cannot identify the offending test without first making all test artifacts traceable, which is itself a large-scale refactoring effort. - **No return on investment:** Time spent making test infrastructure concurrency-safe is time not spent improving the product or adding meaningful test coverage. ## Precedent: Roslyn's Approach The Roslyn team (`dotnet/roslyn`) faced the exact same problem and made the deliberate decision to **disable in-process parallelism** years ago. Their approach is well-established and battle-tested. ### What Roslyn does 1. **Disabled xUnit parallelization**: their `eng/config/xunit.runner.json` sets `"parallelizeTestCollections": false` 2. **Multi-process local execution**: their `RunTests.dll` tool launches multiple subprocesses (`1.5 x processor count`), each running a test assembly in isolation 3. **Helix distribution for CI**: test assemblies are partitioned by historical execution time and distributed across multiple Helix machines ### Why Roslyn disabled parallelism From [roslyn#80095](https://github.com/dotnet/roslyn/issues/80095): > "Naive single-process parallelization wasn't tractable; workspace APIs and native components were problematic." From [roslyn#321](https://github.com/dotnet/roslyn/issues/321): > "xUnit 2.0 will run tests in parallel and several of our tests fail in that mode. Disabling for now." The pattern is identical to what `dotnet/sdk` is experiencing today. ### Roslyn's results By separating parallelism from isolation (run many processes, each sequential), Roslyn achieves: - **Fast CI** through Helix distribution across machines - **Fast local runs** through multi-process execution - **Reliable tests** because each process has full isolation - **No whack-a-mole**: no need to chase concurrency bugs in test infrastructure ## Recommendation ### Immediate action: Disable in-process parallelism 1. Set `parallelizeTestCollections: false` in xUnit runner configuration 2. Set `MSTestParallelizeScope: None` for MSTest projects 3. Validate CI stability improves This disables running multiple tests concurrently within a single process. Tests that are themselves written to exercise concurrency in the product (e.g., testing parallel workloads) are unaffected, as their internal concurrency is not controlled by the test runner. ### Expected outcomes - **Dramatic reduction in intermittent test failures** (expected 90%+) as cross-test contamination is eliminated by isolation. A small number of tests with internal concurrency issues (e.g., [#54960](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/54960)) will remain, but the vast majority of current flakiness is caused by inter-test parallelism. - **CI pass rate recovers** from 3.7% toward a healthy baseline - **`/ba-g` usage drops** as flaky tests stabilize - **Known Build Error backlog shrinks** as root causes are eliminated by isolation - **Developer productivity improves**: less time retrying CI, investigating flakes, filing issues ### Cost The primary concern is **wall-clock execution time**. Disabling in-process parallelism without compensating will make individual test runs slower. However: - Helix already provides machine-level distribution for CI - The current "fast but broken" parallel execution provides **no value** if tests are unreliable; 63% of PRs encounter intermittent test failures, so the speed is illusory **Measured Helix compute impact (PR [#54943](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54943)):** *With parallelism disabled:* | Build | Helix Work Items | Total Compute (machine-hours) | |:------|:-----------------|:------------------------------| | [First run](https://dev.azure.com/dnceng-public/public/_build/results?buildId=1477650) | 693 | 35.85 | | [Second run](https://dev.azure.com/dnceng-public/public/_build/results?buildId=1478060) | 693 | 36.83 | | [Third run](https://dev.azure.com/dnceng-public/public/_build/results?buildId=1479308) | 693 | 37.88 | | **Average** | **693** | **36.85** | *Baseline (parallelism enabled, 5 recent green PRs):* | PR | Helix Work Items | Total Compute (machine-hours) | |:---|:-----------------|:------------------------------| | [#54859](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54859) | 688 | 30.74 | | [#54792](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54792) | 691 | 31.63 | | [#54704](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54704) | 691 | 32.07 | | [#54748](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54748) | 688 | 33.77 | | [#54896](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54896) | 691 | 33.65 | | **Average** | **690** | **32.37** | Average shows a **+4.5 hour (~14%) increase** in total Helix agent compute. This is modest given the stability benefits. ### Long-term: Decide whether to reinvest in concurrent test execution Disabling parallelism solves the immediate crisis. The longer-term question is whether there is sufficient ROI in making the test suite concurrency-safe. This would be a sustained, ongoing investment, not a one-time fix. If we decide the ROI is there, the recommended approach is to re-enable parallelism **on a per-assembly basis**. Some test assemblies are likely better authored for concurrency than others. Rather than an all-or-nothing switch, we would evaluate each assembly individually and opt in only those that demonstrate stability under concurrent execution. This incremental approach limits risk, lets us measure the benefit of each addition, and could recover some or all of the ~14% Helix compute regression by re-enabling parallelism for the assemblies where it works reliably. For other assemblies where concurrency issues are more deeply embedded, the ROI of making them thread-safe may not justify the investment. If we decide the ROI is not there, multi-process execution (as Roslyn does) provides an alternative path to fast local test runs without requiring concurrency-safe test code. Each process runs a test assembly in isolation, and parallelism comes from running many processes simultaneously. This would only apply to the local development experience; in CI, Helix already provides machine-level distribution across work items, so multi-process execution is not needed there. Regardless of the parallelism decision, there are follow-up investments we can make to improve test infrastructure robustness: - **Replace raw `GetTempPath` usage** with a test-aware alternative that provides isolation and traceability - **Write analyzers to catch bad patterns** (e.g., shared mutable state, non-unique temp paths, unsynchronized file access) before they reach CI - These improvements reduce future risk whether or not parallelism is eventually re-enabled ## Conclusion Over 63% of PRs encounter intermittent test failures, and more than a third require `/ba-g` to merge. This is not a sustainable state. The underlying concurrency issues are systemic, difficult to diagnose, and intractable to fix individually. Continuing down the path of addressing these failures one by one is not enough. This problem is affecting us right now, and we need to get it under control. Disabling in-process parallelism eliminates the root cause immediately. Once stability is restored, we can then decide whether the long-term ROI of making the test suite concurrency-safe justifies the sustained investment required, or whether alternative approaches to fast execution better serve the team. ---

Appendix: PR Data

head commit - /ba-g: GitHub issues comments API (`per_page=100`), searched for `/ba-g` substring - Retries: Whether `/azp run` comments were posted after the initial trigger - KBE Auto: Build Analysis Issues.Title: Fix potential assembly mismatches for Microsoft.TestPlatform.ObjectModel
Issues.Body:
Pin Microsoft.TestPlatform.ObjectModel to the in-box vstest version

Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.VSTestBridge (pulled in transitively by test projects using MSTest.Sdk/MTP) requests an older Microsoft.TestPlatform.ObjectModel than the in-box vstest shipped by Microsoft.TestPlatform.CLI. Because test projects build into the test SDK layout, that older ObjectModel (and the CoreUtilities / PlatformAbstractions assemblies in the same package) overwrites the newer in-box vstest assemblies, causing 'dotnet test' (VSTest mode) to crash with MissingMethodException: RunConfiguration.get_CreateNoNewWindow.

With central package transitive pinning enabled, adding a PackageVersion entry pins the transitive ObjectModel to the in-box vstest version so the two stay in sync.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Fix potential assembly mismatches for Microsoft.TestPlatform.ObjectModel" (#54952)

Issues.Title: [automated] Merge branch 'main' => 'release/dnup'
Issues.Body:
I detected changes in the main branch which have not been merged yet to release/dnup. I'm a robot and am configured to help you automatically keep release/dnup up to date, so I've opened this PR. This PR merges commits made on main by the following committers: * Evangelink ## Instructions for merging from UI This PR will not be auto-merged. When pull request checks pass, complete this PR by creating a merge commit, *not* a squash or rebase commit. If this repo does not allow creating merge commits from the GitHub UI, use command line instructions. ## Instructions for merging via command line Run these commands to merge this pull request from the command line. ``` sh git fetch git checkout main git pull --ff-only git checkout release/dnup git pull --ff-only git merge --no-ff main # If there are merge conflicts, resolve them and then run git merge --continue to complete the merge # Pushing the changes to the PR branch will re-trigger PR validation. git push https://github.com/dotnet/sdk HEAD:merge/main-to-release/dnup ```
or if you are using SSH ``` git push git@github.com:dotnet/sdk HEAD:merge/main-to-release/dnup ```
After PR checks are complete push the branch ``` git push ``` ## Instructions for resolving conflicts :warning: If there are merge conflicts, you will need to resolve them manually before merging. You can do this [using GitHub][resolve-github] or using the [command line][resolve-cli]. [resolve-github]: https://help.github.com/articles/resolving-a-merge-conflict-on-github/ [resolve-cli]: https://help.github.com/articles/resolving-a-merge-conflict-using-the-command-line/ ## Instructions for updating this pull request Contributors to this repo have permission update this pull request by pushing to the branch 'merge/main-to-release/dnup'. This can be done to resolve conflicts or make other changes to this pull request before it is merged. The provided examples assume that the remote is named 'origin'. If you have a different remote name, please replace 'origin' with the name of your remote. ``` git fetch git checkout -b merge/main-to-release/dnup origin/release/dnup git pull https://github.com/dotnet/sdk merge/main-to-release/dnup (make changes) git commit -m "Updated PR with my changes" git push https://github.com/dotnet/sdk HEAD:merge/main-to-release/dnup ```
or if you are using SSH ``` git fetch git checkout -b merge/main-to-release/dnup origin/release/dnup git pull git@github.com:dotnet/sdk merge/main-to-release/dnup (make changes) git commit -m "Updated PR with my changes" git push git@github.com:dotnet/sdk HEAD:merge/main-to-release/dnup ```
Contact .NET Core Engineering (dotnet/dnceng) if you have questions or issues. Also, if this PR was generated incorrectly, help us fix it. See https://github.com/dotnet/arcade/blob/main/.github/workflows/scripts/inter-branch-merge.ps1.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "[automated] Merge branch 'main' => 'release/dnup'" (#54966)

Issues.Title: Restore [CombinatorialData] in dotnet-watch.Tests using Combinatorial.MSTest
Issues.Body:
Follow-up to #54903.

That PR migrated `dotnet-watch.Tests` from xUnit to MSTest.Sdk (MTP). As an unwanted side effect, it removed the use of combinatorial test data — every `[CombinatorialData]` method was replaced with hand-expanded `[DataRow(...)]` lists, which are verbose and easy to get out of sync with the parameter space.

This PR restores the combinatorial style using **[`Combinatorial.MSTest`](https://github.com/Youssef1313/Combinatorial.MSTest)** — the MSTest-native port whose attribute surface (`[CombinatorialData]`, `[CombinatorialValues]`, `[CombinatorialRange]`) matches `Xunit.Combinatorial`. This is the same package already consumed by `test/dotnet.Tests`, and its version flows through central package management (`eng/dependabot/Packages.props`). The xUnit `Xunit.Combinatorial` package is left untouched (still used by other projects).

### Changes
- **`dotnet-watch.Tests.csproj`**: add `Combinatorial.MSTest` `PackageReference` and the `Combinatorial.MSTest` global `Using`.
- **13 test files**: convert the affected methods back from `[TestMethod]` + explicit `[DataRow(...)]` lists to `[TestMethod, CombinatorialData]`, restoring `[CombinatorialValues(...)]` / `[CombinatorialRange(...)]` parameter attributes where needed and preserving `[Ignore]` / `[OSCondition]`:
  - BrowserRefreshServerTests, EvaluationTests, CommandLineOptionsTests, LaunchSettingsTests, FileWatcherTests, AutoRestartTests, BuildParametersSelectionPromptTests, BuildProjectsTests, FileExclusionTests, MauiHotReloadTests, MetadataUpdateHandlerTests, ProjectUpdateTests, RuntimeProcessLauncherTests

All other MSTest migration changes from #54903 (the `[TestMethod]` attribute, MSTest assertions, lifecycle, etc.) are kept intact. Methods that originally used `[InlineData]` (now legitimately `[DataRow]`) are left unchanged.

### Validation
`dotnet build test/dotnet-watch.Tests/dotnet-watch.Tests.csproj` — **Build succeeded, 0 warnings, 0 errors.**
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Restore [CombinatorialData] in dotnet-watch.Tests using Combinatorial.MSTest" (#54944)

Issues.Title: Fix potential assembly mismatches for Microsoft.TestPlatform.ObjectModel
Issues.Body:
Pin Microsoft.TestPlatform.ObjectModel to the in-box vstest version.

Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.VSTestBridge (pulled in transitively by test projects using MSTest.Sdk/MTP) requests an older Microsoft.TestPlatform.ObjectModel than the in-box vstest shipped by Microsoft.TestPlatform.CLI. Because test projects build into the test SDK layout, that older ObjectModel (and the CoreUtilities / PlatformAbstractions assemblies in the same package) overwrites the newer in-box vstest assemblies, causing `dotnet test` (VSTest mode) to crash with `MissingMethodException: RunConfiguration.get_CreateNoNewWindow`.

With central package transitive pinning enabled, adding a PackageVersion entry pins the transitive ObjectModel to the in-box vstest version so the two stay in sync.

This is a standalone version of the change in #54952, branched from current `main` (which already contains the unrelated `ToolPackageInstanceTests` build-break fix), so it can land independently of that fork-based PR.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Fix potential assembly mismatches for Microsoft.TestPlatform.ObjectModel" (#54964)

Issues.Title: `dotnetup dotnet` swallows `--help` argument instead of passing it to `dotnet`
Issues.Body:
### Prerequisites

- [x] I have read the [dotnetup documentation](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/tree/release/dnup/documentation/general/dotnetup).
- [x] I have searched for [existing dotnetup issues](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues?q=is%3Aissue%20state%3Aopen%20label%3Adotnetup).
- [x] I have searched for [existing dotnetup discussions](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/discussions/categories/dotnetup).

### Issue type

Bug report

### Description

I will use `dotnet run` as an example command but I think this applies to any `dotnet` CLI command.

When I run `dotnetup dotnet run --help`, I expect to see the same output as `dotnet run --help`:

```
Description:
  .NET Run Command

Usage:
  dotnet run [<applicationArguments>...] [options]

Arguments:
  <applicationArguments>  Arguments passed to the application that is being run. []

Options:
  -c, --configuration <CONFIGURATION>     The configuration to run for. The default for most projects is 'Debug'.
  -f, --framework <FRAMEWORK>             The target framework to run for. The target ...
<snip/>
```

Instead, I see the help output for `dotnetup dotnet`:

```
Description:
  Run a command using the dotnetup-managed dotnet installation.

Usage:
  dotnetup dotnet [options] [[--] <additional arguments>...]]

Options:
  -?, -h, --help  Show help and usage information

Additional Arguments:
  Arguments passed to the application that is being run.
```


### dotnetup version

_No response_
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "`dotnetup dotnet` swallows `--help` argument instead of passing it to `dotnet`" (#54962)

Issues.Title: dotnetup crashes with malformed global.json in the current directory
Issues.Body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/discussions/54839
Originally posted by **jjonescz** June 18, 2026 Create global.json like the following: ```jsonc { "sdk": { // "version": "10.0.100" } } ``` Run `dotnetup` on WSL. Observe: ```sh Error: '/' is invalid after a value. Expected either ',', '}', or ']'. Path: $.sdk | LineNumber: 2 | BytePositionInLine: 8. ``` Not a very clear error, took me a while to figure out what's wrong. `dotnet` itself can handle such `global.json` btw. But even if it was completely malformed, I'm not sure why `dotnetup` should be reading `global.json` from the current directory in the first place.
dotnetup reads global jsons when doing installs/etc - but if the dotnetup command doesn't use the global.json we shouldn't be parsing it.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "dotnetup crashes with malformed global.json in the current directory" (#54961)

Issues.Title: Disable failing Mark-of-the-Web test and fix parallel test failures
Issues.Body:
Three independent test-stabilization fixes found while triaging #54871's CI failures:

 - **Disable the Mark-of-the-Web test** (#54951): `DangerousFileDetectorTests.ItShouldDetectFileWithMarkOfTheWeb` can't  pass on IE-less Windows images — urlmon `MapUrlToZone` ignores the MOTW stream, and the product `IsDangerous` and a  capability probe diverge — so it's `[Ignore]`d against #54951.
 - **Serialize `DependencyProviderTests`**: its methods share a hardcoded registry key and race under MSTest  `MethodLevel` parallelism (#54766). Added class-level `[DoNotParallelize]`.
 - **Fix `ILLink_TrimMode_new_options` binlog collision**: the parallel `full`/`partial` DataRows both wrote  `msbuild.binlog` to the shared work-item directory (`MSB4104`). Set a per-DataRow working directory.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Disable failing Mark-of-the-Web test and fix parallel test failures" (#54945)

Issues.Title: [DRAFT] Consume Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Internal.DotnetTest source package
Issues.Body:
> **Draft / discovery PR — does not build yet, intentionally.** It exists to drive out every concrete gap in consuming the [`Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Internal.DotnetTest`](https://github.com/microsoft/testfx/tree/main/src/Platform/Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Internal.DotnetTest) source-only package instead of hand-copying its files into `dotnet test`.

## What this does

`Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Internal.DotnetTest` is an **internal, source-only** NuGet from microsoft/testfx. It ships — as `contentFiles/cs/any` compiled into the consumer — the pieces of the `dotnet test` ↔ Microsoft.Testing.Platform integration that must stay a single source of truth across microsoft/testfx and dotnet/sdk:

- the named-pipe **wire contract** (`ObjectFieldIds.cs`, `Constants.cs`), and
- the **terminal reporter** (reporter + rendering + state + small platform abstractions) plus its localized `TerminalResources` (resx/xlf, wired via the package's `build/` props).

Today dotnet/sdk hand-maintains its own copies under `src/Cli/dotnet/Commands/Test/MTP/` (the `ObjectFieldIds.cs` even carries a *"must be kept aligned with the testfx repo"* warning). This PR starts replacing those copies with the package:

- Add `PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Internal.DotnetTest"` to `src/Cli/dotnet/dotnet.csproj` + register it in `Directory.Packages.props` (CPM).
- Add `CS0436` to `NoWarn` (required — see Gap 4).
- Delete `Commands/Test/MTP/IPC/ObjectFieldIds.cs` and the entire `Commands/Test/MTP/Terminal/` reporter copy.

## Missing pieces this PR surfaces (why it doesn't build yet)

**1. The package must be published + flowed before it can be restored (prerequisite).**
On the testfx side the package is being promoted to `IsShipping=true` (signed, **always-preview** via `SuppressFinalPackageVersion`), so it publishes on the public test-tools feed on the same `2.3.0-preview.*` train as the rest of Microsoft.Testing.Platform — the `Internal` name + `-preview` suffix keep it clearly an implementation detail. Until a testfx build that produces it has flowed into this repo, the version here (`2.3.0-preview.26321.1`, matching the `Microsoft.Testing.Platform` dependency already in `eng/Version.Details.xml`) is not yet resolvable; once it flows it should be added as a darc dependency next to `Microsoft.Testing.Platform` and referenced via the generated property. **This is the prerequisite to everything else.**

**2. Namespace divergence (every call site).**
The shared source lives in `Microsoft.Testing.Platform.IPC` and `Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal`. dotnet/sdk's copies live in `Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.Commands.Test.IPC` and `Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.Commands.Test.Terminal`, and consumers (`TestApplicationHandler`, `TestApplication`, …) reference them via `using Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.Commands.Test.Terminal;` and types like `Terminal.FlatException`, `TerminalTestReporter`. After consuming the package these must be re-pointed to the testfx namespaces (or bridged with `global using` aliases). The `[Embedded]` types resolve cleanly; the non-embedded abstractions need Gap 4.

**3. The wire contract is only partially shared — `Constants` + models + serializers are not.**
The package shares `ObjectFieldIds.cs` (deleted here) **and** `Constants.cs`, but dotnet/sdk's protocol constants live mixed into `Commands/Test/CliConstants.cs` (CLI-specific), so there is no clean file to delete — consuming the shared `Constants.cs` requires splitting `CliConstants`. Additionally, by design the package does **not** yet share the message **models** (`IPC/Models/`), **serializers** (`IPC/Serializers/`), or the NamedPipe transport (`NamedPipe*`, `IRequest/IResponse`) — those still depend on `TestMetadataProperty` and use a different class shape. So those stay hand-copied and remain a drift risk (kept in this PR).

**4. Terminal reporter has already diverged (bidirectional).**
The package's reporter has been refactored relative to dotnet/sdk's copy:
- testfx-only (the package brings these in): `TerminalTestReporter.*` partials, `IProgressRenderer`, `CursorProgressRenderer`, `SilenceDrivenHeartbeatRenderer`, `RoslynString`, `ApplicationStateGuard`, `StackTraceHelper`, `TestRunSummaryHelper`.
- sdk-only (deleted here, **no package equivalent**): `ErrorMessage.cs`, `WarningMessage.cs`, `IProgressMessage.cs` — call sites depending on these must be reconciled to the new shape.
Because some abstractions (`IConsole/IStopwatch/IColor/SystemConsole/SystemConsoleColor/SystemStopwatch`) are **not** `[Embedded]`, a consumer that also sees `Microsoft.Testing.Platform` gets benign `CS0436` warnings; this repo's `TreatWarningsAsErrors=true` turns them into errors, hence the `NoWarn;CS0436` added here.

**5. No end-to-end validation yet.**
Once the package flows and the above are reconciled, we need a green build of `src/Cli/dotnet` against the package and a run of the existing `dotnet test` E2E suite to prove identical wire behavior + reporter output.

## Consumer prerequisites — already satisfied in this repo ✅
- `ImplicitUsings=enable` (root `Directory.Build.props`) — package `build/` props supply the reporter's extra global usings.
- `LangVersion=Preview` — needed for the `field` keyword the reporter uses.
- `Microsoft.CodeAnalysis.EmbeddedAttribute` — already defined in-repo.
- `XliffTasks` (via Arcade) — for the localized `TerminalResources` satellites.

## Suggested order to make it real
1. (testfx) Promote the package to `IsShipping=true` (preview) so it publishes + flows to dotnet/sdk → unblocks Gap 1. *(in progress — microsoft/testfx)*
2. (this repo) Add the darc dependency + reference the flowed version; re-point namespaces (Gap 2); reconcile `CliConstants`/reporter shape (Gaps 3–4).
3. Build + run `dotnet test` E2E (Gap 5).

Issues.Title: Default MSTest parallelization to class level
Issues.Body:
## What

Change the default `MSTestParallelizeScope` from `MethodLevel` to `ClassLevel` in `test/Directory.Build.props`, and clean up the `[DoNotParallelize]` attributes that were guarding against the intra-class method races that method-level parallelism caused.

Under `ClassLevel`, test methods within a class run sequentially while different classes run in parallel — which removes the need for most of the per-class `[DoNotParallelize]` opt-outs.

## Changes

- **Default scope → `ClassLevel`** in `test/Directory.Build.props` (applies to projects using `MSTest.Sdk` that don't override the scope).
- **Removed all `[DoNotParallelize]` attributes** — 54 occurrences:
  - class-level `[DoNotParallelize]`
  - assembly-level `[assembly: DoNotParallelize]`
- **Deleted** `test/dotnet-aot.Tests/AssemblyInfo.cs` and `test/dotnet-format.UnitTests/AssemblyInfo.cs`, which contained only the assembly attribute.
- **Removed orphaned comments** that explained the removed attributes, and collapsed the blank lines they left.
- **Reworded** one stale comment that referenced "method-level parallelization (MSTest.Sdk default)".

### Intentionally kept
- The two **method-level** `[DoNotParallelize]` attributes in `ComputeReferenceStaticWebAssetItemsTest.cs` and `GenerateStaticWebAssetEndpointsPropsFileTest.cs` — they guard individual tests that mutate the process current directory.
- All **project-level** `MSTestParallelizeScope` overrides (`None`/empty/explicit `ClassLevel`) are left untouched, to be revisited per-project later.

## ⚠️ Behavioral risk to watch

Projects with **no** `MSTestParallelizeScope` override now inherit the new `ClassLevel` default. Several of these previously relied on `[DoNotParallelize]` for **cross-class** isolation (classes now run in parallel with each other):

- `Microsoft.DotNet.PackageInstall.Tests` (mutate `PATH`/env + shared tool cache)
- `Microsoft.NET.Build.Containers.UnitTests` (env vars + static auth cache)
- `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Razor.Tool.Tests`, `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Publish.Tasks.Tests` (env vars)
- `Microsoft.TemplateEngine.Cli.UnitTests` (static `Reporter`)
- `Microsoft.NET.ToolPack.Tests`, `dotnet-MsiInstallation.Tests` (VM)

If any of these become flaky, the per-project knob is to set that project's `MSTestParallelizeScope` to `None`.

Issues.Title: Fix main build break: convert ToolPackageInstanceTests to MSTest (#54822)
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Fixes the current `main` build break in `Microsoft.DotNet.PackageInstall.Tests`.

`ToolPackageInstanceTests.cs` (added by #54822) uses the xUnit `[Fact]` attribute, but the project was migrated to **MSTest.Sdk** (`Migrate Microsoft.DotNet.PackageInstall.Tests to MSTest.Sdk on MTP`) and no longer references xUnit, so `[Fact]` fails to compile:

```
ToolPackageInstanceTests.cs(11,10): error CS0246: The type or namespace name 'Fact' could not be found
```

This converts the test to `[TestClass]`/`[TestMethod]` to match the rest of the (MSTest) test project.

## Root cause

A stale-base merge race: #54822 was validated on a pre-migration (xUnit) base, the MSTest migration merged in between, and #54822 was squash-merged later without re-running CI against current `main`. The change doesn't textually conflict, so no merge conflict surfaced and the incompatibility wasn't caught.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Closed or merged PR "Fix main build break: convert ToolPackageInstanceTests to MSTest (#54822)" (#54954)

Issues.Title: Consider changing MSTest parallelization from MethodLevel to ClassLevel
Issues.Body:
# Summary

xUnit tests by default use class-level parallelism.  The migration to MSTest switched to MethodLevel parallelism, which has the potential to break tests.  I ran into two cases of this today and worked around the issue by adding `[DoNotParallelize]` in https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54945.

Is there a good reason to change the parallelism when migrating to MSTest?  With a large library of tests that were written assuming class-level parallelism, changing to method level parallelism seems rather risky and likely to introduce flakiness.

# Copilot's verbose description

[#54766](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54766) set `MSTestParallelizeScope` to `MethodLevel` repo-wide (`test/Directory.Build.props`). This runs **test methods (and individual `[DataRow]` cases) within a class concurrently**, which is a semantic change from the previous xUnit behavior where methods within a class ran **serially**. Tests that relied on that implicit intra-class serialization now race on shared state.

```xml
<!-- test/Directory.Build.props -->
<MSTestParallelizeScope>MethodLevel</MSTestParallelizeScope>
```

## Why this is a problem

xUnit parallelized at the **collection/class** level but ran the methods *inside* a class one at a time. MSTest `MethodLevel` parallelizes individual methods/data rows, so any test class that shares process- or machine-global state between its methods can now interleave and fail intermittently.

## Confirmed casualties (already fixed)

- **`DependencyProviderTests`** (`Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.Utils.Tests`) — three methods create/mutate the same hardcoded registry key `.NET_SDK_TEST_PROVIDER_KEY`; under MethodLevel they collide (`expected 1, actual 2 dependents`). Fixed with class-level `[DoNotParallelize]` in #54945.
- **`ILLink_TrimMode_new_options`** (`Microsoft.NET.Publish.Tests`) — the `full`/`partial` `[DataRow]`s run concurrently and both pass a bare `-bl`, writing `msbuild.binlog` to the shared work-item working directory → `MSB4104: ... being used by another process`. Fixed by setting a per-DataRow working directory in #54945.

## Latent risk

Only **5 of 54** MSTest.Sdk test assemblies are protected with `[assembly: DoNotParallelize]` (`dotnet-aot.Tests`, `dotnet-format.UnitTests`, `dotnet.Tests`, `EndToEnd.Tests`, `Microsoft.DotNet.MSBuildSdkResolver.Tests`); the other **49 inherit MethodLevel**. Common hazards that are not yet addressed:

- **~51 tests pass a bare `-bl`**, all writing `msbuild.binlog` to the same current directory (the same hazard as `ILLink_TrimMode_new_options`, just not yet triggered).
- Tests that mutate **process-global state** (`Environment.SetEnvironmentVariable`, `Directory.SetCurrentDirectory`) with save/restore patterns that are not thread-safe (e.g. `GivenThatWeWantToBuildWithGlobalJson` sets `MSBUILDINCLUDEDEFAULTSDKRESOLVER`).
- Shared machine state (registry keys, fixed temp paths).

## Proposed options

1. **(Recommended) Default to `ClassLevel`** — restores the xUnit guarantee that methods/data rows within a class don't run concurrently, eliminating most of these races for free, while still parallelizing across classes. Opt into `MethodLevel` per-assembly where verified safe.
2. **Keep `MethodLevel`** but systematically audit and fix shared-state patterns: unique `-bl` paths / per-test working directories, unique registry keys/temp paths, and avoiding process-global mutations (or guarding the affected classes with `[DoNotParallelize]`).

## References

- #54766 (introduced `MethodLevel`)
- #54945 (fixes for the two confirmed casualties)
- `test/Directory.Build.props` (the setting)
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "Consider changing MSTest parallelization from MethodLevel to ClassLevel" (#54956)

Issues.Title: Stabilize flaky dotnet-watch tests (DeleteSubfolder, ServerAndResources)
Issues.Body:
## Summary

Attempt to fix two flaky `dotnet-watch` tests.  This is based on copilot's analysis of failures seen in https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/pull/54945.  One of them also failed on an unrelated PR.  Pipelines:

- https://dev.azure.com/dnceng-public/public/_build/results?buildId=1478226
- https://dev.azure.com/dnceng-public/public/_build/results?buildId=1478247

Copilot's descriptions below:

### `FileWatcherTests.DeleteSubfolder`
On macOS the file-system event replay intermittently includes a directory-level `subdir` `Add` event. Because the test stops collecting once it has seen the expected number of events, that spurious event displaces one of the expected file events and the assertion fails. Restrict the watcher to the watched file names (`foo1`/`foo2`/`foo3`) so the directory event is filtered out by the watcher's existing name filter. Harmless on other platforms.

### `AspireLauncherIntegrationTests.ServerAndResources`
Status events are delivered over a separate pipe from the server's output. The test cancelled the status reader based on the server output, which races with status delivery and occasionally dropped the restart's `process_started` event. `ReadStatusEventsAsync` now reads until the expected number of status events has been received instead of relying on output-based cancellation.

Related: #53061 (the existing macOS tracking issue for this test; the `[OSCondition(Exclude)]` is left in place for the watch team to re-evaluate).

## Notes

These touch nondeterministic file-watcher / launcher tests, so they're proposed for watch-team review.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Stabilize flaky dotnet-watch tests (DeleteSubfolder, ServerAndResources)" (#54955)

Issues.Title: .NET 10 dotnet pack chokes on output folder named dotnet
Issues.Body:
### Describe the bug

When specifying the output folder of `dotnet pack` with `-o`, if the folder is named "dotnet", it will crash with obscure error. Either MSB1009 when project file is not specified, or MSB1008 when project file is specified.

### Steps to reproduce

I run the test in docker image mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:10.0.301-resolute-amd64
```
root@ec505c3cd615:~# cd /mnt
root@ec505c3cd615:/mnt# mkdir test
root@ec505c3cd615:/mnt# cd test/
root@ec505c3cd615:/mnt/test# dotnet new classlib

root@ec505c3cd615:/mnt/test# dotnet pack -o /mnt/dotnet

Switch: PackageOutputPath=/mnt/dotnet
root@ec505c3cd615:/mnt/test# dotnet pack test.csproj -o /mnt/dotnet
MSBUILD : error MSB1008: Only one project can be specified.
    Full command line: '/usr/share/dotnet/sdk/10.0.301/MSBuild -maxcpucount --verbosity:m -tlp:default=auto --property:NuGetInteractive=true --property:_IsPacking=true --property:Configuration=Release --restoreProperty:NuGetInteractive=true --restoreProperty:_IsPacking=true --restoreProperty:Configuration=Release --restoreProperty:EnableDefaultCompileItems=false --restoreProperty:EnableDefaultEmbeddedResourceItems=false --restoreProperty:EnableDefaultNoneItems=false --target:Pack --nologo PackageOutputPath=/mnt/dotnet test.csproj -restore -distributedlogger:Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.Commands.MSBuild.MSBuildLogger,/usr/share/dotnet/sdk/10.0.301/dotnet.dll*Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.Commands.MSBuild.MSBuildForwardingLogger,/usr/share/dotnet/sdk/10.0.301/dotnet.dll'

### Actual behavior

### Is this a regression?

Yes, it works fine with .NET 9 and 11 preview.

Issues.Title: DangerousFileDetectorTests.ItShouldDetectFileWithMarkOfTheWeb fails on IE-less vs2026.pre.scout image
Issues.Body:
### Summary

`Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.Utils.Tests.DangerousFileDetectorTests.ItShouldDetectFileWithMarkOfTheWeb` fails on the new **`windows.amd64.vs2026.pre.scout`** CI image (Windows Server 2025 + VS 2026 preview, with Internet Explorer removed).

```
Expected boolean to be True, but found False.
```

### Root cause

The test writes a `Zone.Identifier` alternate data stream (`ZoneId=3`, Internet zone) and asserts that `DangerousFileDetector.IsDangerous(file)` returns `true`. `DangerousFileDetector` relies on the legacy IE/urlmon `InternetSecurityManager` and `MapUrlToZone`.

On the IE-stripped scout image this is **unreliable and inconsistent**:

- The `InternetSecurityManager` COM class still instantiates (urlmon is present), but `MapUrlToZone` no longer reliably honors the `Zone.Identifier` stream.
- Worse, the behavior **diverges between code paths**: an independent `MapUrlToZone` probe can report the file as Internet zone (`zone >= URLZONE_INTERNET`) while the product's `DangerousFileDetector.IsDangerous` (CsWin32 path) reports it as *not* dangerous on the same image. So capability-gating the assertion on an independent probe does not work — the gate and the product disagree.

### Repro

- Build [1477806](https://dev.azure.com/dnceng-public/public/_build/results?buildId=1477806) (PR #54945), `TestBuild: windows (x64)` leg on `windows.amd64.vs2026.pre.scout.open`.

### Mitigation

Disabling the test on the affected image via `[Ignore]` pointing at this issue.

### Follow-up

Decide on a robust long-term approach, e.g.:
- Assert only the deterministic negative case (a file without Mark-of-the-Web is never dangerous), and drop the environment-dependent positive assertion; or
- Reliably detect functional Mark-of-the-Web support before asserting; or
- Skip the positive assertion on images where the IE/urlmon zone manager is non-functional.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open issue "DangerousFileDetectorTests.ItShouldDetectFileWithMarkOfTheWeb fails on IE-less vs2026.pre.scout image" (#54951)

Issues.Title: Consolidate duplicate MSBuildUtilities copies (#51487)
Issues.Body:
## Why

`MSBuildUtilities` existed as two copies whose class bodies were byte-for-byte identical, differing only by namespace:

- `src/Common/MSBuildUtilities.cs` in `Microsoft.DotNet.Cli`, compiled into the SDK MSBuild build tasks.
- `src/Cli/Microsoft.DotNet.FileBasedPrograms/MSBuildUtilities.cs` in `Microsoft.DotNet.FileBasedPrograms`, shipped in the source package and consumed by Roslyn, the dotnet CLI, and ProjectTools.

Keeping two copies in sync is exactly the duplication tracked by #51487.

## What

Collapse the two copies into the single physical file `src/Common/MSBuildUtilities.cs` and switch the namespace at compile time with an `MSBUILD_BUILD_TASKS` symbol:

- The default form (no symbol) is the one that originally shipped in the source package: namespace `Microsoft.DotNet.FileBasedPrograms`, with `#nullable enable` and `using System;`. External and uncontrolled consumers (Roslyn, the dotnet CLI) compile the shipped contentFiles with no symbols defined, so this default keeps their input unchanged.
- The two build-tasks projects (`Microsoft.NET.Build.Tasks` and `Microsoft.NET.Build.Extensions.Tasks`) define `MSBUILD_BUILD_TASKS`, which selects the `Microsoft.DotNet.Cli` namespace and drops the nullable/using directives. Those projects enable nullable and provide `System` via ImplicitUsings, so emitting the directives there would otherwise trip IDE0240/IDE0005.

The class body is unchanged, so the emitted IL for `ConvertStringToBool` is identical to before. Net result is `+27 / -72` with one shared file instead of two.

## Validation

- Built every consumer clean (0/0): both build-tasks projects (net11.0 + net472), `Microsoft.DotNet.ProjectTools`, the `Microsoft.DotNet.FileBasedPrograms` source package, and the dotnet CLI.
- Inspected the compiled assemblies' metadata to confirm per-assembly namespaces: the two build-tasks DLLs expose `Microsoft.DotNet.Cli.MSBuildUtilities`; `Microsoft.DotNet.ProjectTools.dll` exposes `Microsoft.DotNet.FileBasedPrograms.MSBuildUtilities`.
- Confirmed the shipped source package still packs `MSBuildUtilities.cs` into `contentFiles/cs/{net9.0,netstandard2.0}/` defaulting to the `Microsoft.DotNet.FileBasedPrograms` namespace, matching the prior shipped contract.
- Ran the build-tasks unit tests that exercise `ConvertStringToBool` (`ProcessFrameworkReferences`: 39/39 passing).

`InternalAPI.Unshipped.txt` is unchanged because the default namespace remains `Microsoft.DotNet.FileBasedPrograms`.

Contributes to #51487
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Consolidate duplicate MSBuildUtilities copies (#51487)" (#54947)

Issues.Title: Disable in-process parallel test execution
Issues.Body:
## Purpose

Disable in-process parallel test execution to address widespread concurrency-related intermittent test failures.

## Changes

- `test/xunit.runner.json`: Set `parallelizeAssembly: false` and `parallelizeTestCollections: false`
- `test/TemplateEngine/.../xunit.runner.json`: Same
- `test/Directory.Build.props`: Set `MSTestParallelizeScope=None`

## Context

See #54967 for the full analysis and data supporting this change.

Closes #54967

Issues.Title: Static Web Assets: apply deferred asset group resolution during publish
Issues.Body:
Deferred Static Web Asset groups were resolved during `build` but never during `publish`. When two grouped variants resolved to the same route with `AssetKind=All` (e.g. a package fallback plus an app-generated asset), the variant the group was supposed to exclude survived into the publish pipeline and `GenerateStaticWebAssetEndpointsManifest` threw `InvalidOperationException: Sequence contains more than one element`. The same project built successfully.

## Root cause

The build manifest intentionally retains *all* group variants so transitive consumers can re-resolve against their own graph. Publish reloads that manifest but:
1. never re-resolves deferred groups (no publish-side `FilterDeferredStaticWebAssetGroups`), and
2. its `FilterStaticWebAssetGroups` is consumer-scoped (`Source="$(PackageId)"`), so it structurally cannot filter groups owned by a referenced project or NuGet package.

Re-resolving at publish from the package can't fix this — the decision has to be made where the filter is unscoped (build) and carried forward.

## Changes

- **Persist resolved groups in the build manifest** — `StaticWebAssetGroup` is now public/serializable with round-trip (`ToTaskItem`/`FromItemGroupToArray`) and value equality; `StaticAssetsManifest` gains a `Groups` property wired into `Create`/hash/equals. `GenerateStaticWebAssetsManifest` writes the resolved (no-longer-`Deferred`) groups into `staticwebassets.build.json`.
- **Surface groups on read** — `ReadStaticWebAssetsManifestFile` exposes a new `StaticWebAssetGroups` output.
- **Re-apply the unscoped decision at publish** — `LoadStaticWebAssetsBuildManifest` re-runs `FilterStaticWebAssetGroups` (unscoped) using the persisted groups, dropping the excluded variant before the publish endpoints manifest is computed. Build-time groups are also cached (`_CachedBuildStaticWebAssetGroups`) so the in-process build+publish path is covered alongside fresh/no-build publishes that read from the manifest.
- **Regression test** — adds a `publish` case to `DeferredAssetGroupsIntegrationTest` where two `AssetKind=All` variants collapse to one route, owned by a referenced project, and asserts exactly one asset/endpoint survives.

Because the build manifest still retains all variants, transitive consumers continue to re-import the deferred group definitions and re-resolve against their own graph; persistence only governs the current project's build→publish consistency.
Issues.RelatedDescription: Open PR "Static Web Assets: apply deferred asset group resolution during publish" (#54941)

> This issue is a locked mirror of https://github.com/dotnet/core/discussions/10445. See that thread for discussion.

We are pleased to announce the release of .NET 11 Preview 5. Follow the main announcement and [blog announcement](https://aka.ms/dotnet/11/preview5).

Browse the release notes for a full list of improvements in this release:

- [.NET MAUI](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/dotnetmaui.md)
- [ASP.NET Core](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview5/aspnetcore.md)

Issues.Title: Microsoft Security Advisory CVE-2026-45591 | ASP.NET Core Denial of Service Vulnerability
Issues.Body: ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in ASP.NET Core SignalR and Blazor Server. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A denial of service vulnerability exists in the MessagePack hub protocol used by SignalR and Blazor Server where an attacker can send deeply-nested MessagePack arrays to cause a stack overflow, resulting in a denial of service. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/discussions/67100 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** High [Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.AspNetCore.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.27 | 8.0.28 ## Advisory FAQ ### How do I know if I am affected? If using a package listed in [affected packages](#affected-packages), you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET 10.0, .NET 9.0, or .NET 8.0. If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. * If you're using .NET 8.0, you should download and install .NET 8.0.28 Runtime or .NET 8.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0. * If you're using .NET 9.0, you should download and install .NET 9.0.17 Runtime or .NET 9.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/9.0. * If you're using .NET 10.0, you should download and install .NET 10.0.9 Runtime or .NET 10.0.xxx SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/10.0. Once you have installed the updated runtime or SDK, restart your apps for the update to take effect. Additionally, if you've deployed [self-contained applications](https://docs.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/deploying/#self-contained-deployments-scd) targeting any of the impacted versions, these applications are also vulnerable and must be recompiled and redeployed. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in a supported version of .NET, please report it to the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) via the [MSRC Researcher Portal](https://msrc.microsoft.com/report/vulnerability/new). Further information can be found in the MSRC [Report an Issue FAQ](https://www.microsoft.com/msrc/faqs-report-an-issue). Security reports made through MSRC may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Bounty. Details of the Microsoft .NET Bounty Program including terms and conditions are at https://aka.ms/corebounty. ### Support You can ask questions about this issue on GitHub in the .NET GitHub organization. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. You can ask questions in the linked discussion issue. ### Disclaimer The information provided in this advisory is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. Issues.Title: Microsoft Security Advisory CVE-2026-45491 | .NET Tampering Vulnerability
Issues.Body: ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in System.Formats.Tar. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A tampering vulnerability exists in the `TarFile.ExtractToDirectory` method where a symlink path traversal enables an attacker to perform arbitrary file writes outside the intended extraction directory. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/issues/129184 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** Medium - **Score:** 6.8 - **Vector:** `CVSS:3.1/AV:L/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:L` - **Weakness:** CWE-59 (Improper Link Resolution Before File Access) ## Affected Platforms - **Platforms:** All - **Architectures:** All ## Issues.Title: Microsoft Security Advisory CVE-2026-45490 | .NET SDK Elevation of Privilege Vulnerability
Issues.Body: ## Executive summary Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, and .NET 10.0. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their environments to remove this vulnerability. An elevation of privilege vulnerability exists in the .NET SDK `dotnet.exe` workload command where one local user can exploit a weakly ACLed named pipe to perform arbitrary file creation or truncation as another local user. This only affects .NET on Windows operating systems. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/issues/54652 ## Mitigation factors Microsoft has not identified any mitigating factors for this vulnerability. ## Affected software * .NET 10.0 SDK |Affected versions|Patched versions| |-----------------|----------------| |>= 10.0.100, <= 10.0.108|10.0.109| |>= 10.0.300, <= 10.0.300|10.0.301| * .NET 9.0 SDK |Affected versions|Patched versions| |-----------------|----------------| |>= 9.0.100, <= 9.0.117|9.0.118| |>= 9.0.300, <= 9.0.314|9.0.315| * .NET 8.0 SDK |Affected versions|Patched versions| |-----------------|----------------| |>= 8.0.100, <= 8.0.127|8.0.128| |>= 8.0.400, <= 8.0.421|8.0.422| ## Affected Packages The vulnerability does not affect Microsoft .NET packages. ## Advisory FAQ ### How do I know if I am affected? If you have an SDK with a version listed, you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET 8.0 SDK, .NET 9.0 SDK, or .NET 10.0 SDK, as appropriate. * If you're using .NET 8.0, you should download and install the latest .NET 8.0 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/8.0. * If you're using .NET 9.0, you should download and install the latest .NET 9.0 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/9.0. * If you're using .NET 10.0, you should download and install the latest .NET 10.0 SDK from https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet-core/10.0. * You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. You will see output like the following; ``` .NET SDK: Version: 10.0.100 Commit: 59db016f11 Runtime Environment: OS Name: Windows OS Version: 10.0.26100 OS Platform: Windows RID: win-x64 Base Path: C:\Program Files\dotnet\sdk\10.0.100\ .NET SDKs installed: 10.0.100 [C:\Program Files\dotnet\sdk] .NET runtimes installed: Microsoft.AspNetCore.App 10.0.0 [C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.AspNetCore.App] Microsoft.NETCore.App 10.0.0 [C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.NETCore.App] ``` Once you have installed the updated SDK, close any open command prompts, terminals, IDEs, or build agents that may have loaded an older `dotnet.exe`, and then re-run the affected `dotnet workload` command using the updated SDK. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, or .NET 10.0, please email details to [secure@microsoft.com](mailto:secure@microsoft.com). Reports may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Core & .NET 5 Bounty. Details of the Microsoft .NET Bounty Program including terms and conditions are at https://aka.ms/corebounty. ### Support You can ask questions about this issue on GitHub in the .NET GitHub organization. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/sdk. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. You can ask questions in the linked discussion issue. ### Disclaimer The information provided in this advisory is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. Microsoft disclaims all warranties, either express or implied, including the warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose. In no event shall Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers be liable for any damages whatsoever including direct, indirect, incidental, consequential, loss of business profits or special damages, even if Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers have been advised of the possibility of such damages. Some states do not allow the exclusion or limitation of liability for consequential or incidental damages so the foregoing limitation may not apply. ### External Links [CVE-2026-45490](https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-45490) ### Acknowledgements Ky0toFu ### Revisions V1.0 (June 9, 2026): Advisory published.

Issues.Title: .NET 11 Preview 4
Issues.Body:
> This issue is a locked mirror of https://github.com/dotnet/core/discussions/10415. See that thread for discussion.

We are pleased to announce the release of .NET 11 Preview 4. Follow the main announcement and [blog announcement](https://aka.ms/dotnet/11/preview4).

Browse the release notes for a full list of improvements in this release:

- [.NET MAUI](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/dotnetmaui.md)
- [ASP.NET Core](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/aspnetcore.md)
- [C#](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/csharp.md)
- [F#](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/fsharp.md)
- [Container images](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/containers.md)
- [EF Core & Data](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/efcore.md)
- [Libraries](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/libraries.md)
- [MSBuild](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/msbuild.md)
- [NuGet](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/nuget.md)
- [Runtime](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/runtime.md)
- [SDK](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/sdk.md)
- [Windows Forms](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/winforms.md)
- [WPF](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/main/release-notes/11.0/preview/preview4/wpf.md)

## Get started

To get started with .NET 11, [install the .NET 11 SDK](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/download/dotnet/11.0).

If you're on Windows using Visual Studio, we recommend installing the latest [Visual Studio 2026 Insiders](https://visualstudio.microsoft.com/insiders/). You can also use Visual Studio Code and the [C# Dev Kit](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=ms-dotnettools.csdevkit) extension with .NET 11.

Issues.Title: Microsoft Security Advisory CVE-2026-32177 – .NET Elevation of Privilege Vulnerability
Issues.Body: ## Executive Summary: Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, and .NET 10.0. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. Heap-based buffer overflow in .NET allows an unauthorized attacker to elevate privileges locally. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/wpf/issues/11632 ## CVSS Details - **Version:** 3.1 - **Severity:** High - **Score:** 7.3 - **Vector:** AV:L/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:L/E:U/RL:O/RC:C - **Weakness:** CWE-122: Heap-based Buffer Overflow, CWE-20: Improper Input Validation ## Affected Platforms - **Platforms:** Windows - **Architectures:** All ## Affected Packages The vulnerability affects any Microsoft .NET project if it uses any of affected package versions listed below ### .NET 10 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------- [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.7 | 10.0.8 [MMicrosoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.7 | 10.0.8 [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 10.0.0, <= 10.0.7 | 10.0.8 ### .NET 9 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------- [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.15 | 9.0.16 [MMicrosoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.15 | 9.0.16 [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 9.0.0, <= 9.0.15 | 9.0.16 ### .NET 8 Package name | Affected version | Patched version ------------ | ---------------- | ------------------------- [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-arm64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.26 | 8.0.27 [MMicrosoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x64) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.26 | 8.0.27 [Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.WindowsDesktop.App.Runtime.win-x86) | >= 8.0.0, <= 8.0.26 | 8.0.27 ## Advisory FAQ ### How do I know if I am affected? If using a package listed in [affected packages](#affected-packages), you're exposed to the vulnerability. ### How do I fix the issue? 1. To fix the issue please install the latest version of .NET 8.0, NET 9.0, or .NET 10.0, as appropriate. If you have installed one or more .NET SDKs through Visual Studio, Visual Studio will prompt you to update Visual Studio, which will also update your .NET SDKs. 2. If your application references the vulnerable package, update the package reference to the patched version. You can list the versions you have installed by running the `dotnet --info` command. Once you have installed the updated runtime or SDK, restart your apps for the update to take effect. Additionally, if you've deployed [self-contained applications](https://docs.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/deploying/#self-contained-deployments-scd) targeting any of the impacted versions, these applications are also vulnerable and must be recompiled and redeployed. ## Other Information ### Reporting Security Issues If you have found a potential security issue in a supported version of .NET, please report it to the Microsoft Security Response Center (MSRC) via the [MSRC Researcher Portal](https://msrc.microsoft.com/report/vulnerability/new). Further information can be found in the MSRC [Report an Issue FAQ](https://www.microsoft.com/msrc/faqs-report-an-issue). Security reports made through MSRC may qualify for the Microsoft .NET Bounty. Details of the Microsoft .NET Bounty Program including terms and conditions are at https://aka.ms/corebounty. ### Support You can ask questions about this issue on GitHub in the .NET GitHub organization. The main repos are located at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime. The Announcements repo (https://github.com/dotnet/Announcements) will contain this bulletin as an issue and will include a link to a discussion issue. You can ask questions in the linked discussion issue. ### Disclaimer The information provided in this advisory is provided "as is" without warranty of any kind. In no event shall Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers be liable for any damages whatsoever including direct, indirect, incidental, consequential, loss of business profits or special damages, even if Microsoft Corporation or its suppliers have been advised of the possibility of such damages. Issues.Title: Microsoft Security Advisory CVE-2026-32175 – .NET Core Tampering Vulnerability
Issues.Body: ## Executive Summary: Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to provide information about a vulnerability in .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, and .NET 10.0. This advisory also provides guidance on what developers can do to update their applications to remove this vulnerability. A tampering vulnerability exists when .NET Core improperly handles specially crafted files. An attacker who successfully exploited this vulnerability could write arbitrary files and directories to certain locations on a vulnerable system. However, an attacker would have limited control over the destination of the files and directories. To exploit the vulnerability, an attacker must send a specially crafted file to a vulnerable system. The security update fixes the vulnerability by ensuring .NET Core properly handles files. ## Discussion Discussion for this issue can be found at https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/issues/128089 ##
